sentences.text: OK.
Of course, this is the first I had ever heard of a-- of a gas chamber, other than the ones that we read about in the States.
Uh, and I don't think I knew much about them.

But anyhow, the curiosity was not so much for me, you know, as it-- as it was from this other fellow who-- who decided there's no way that that could be a shower, because it said 'shower' on the sign.
And there were shower heads inside, you know, coming out of the wall.
And so, you know, 25 or 30 people could take a shower at the same time.
And-- but he fooled around with it and he could not find any plumbing.
And that's what-- he said to me, there's no way-- and we were having a cigarette or something-- there's no way that-- that you could take a shower in there.
Even though they had already told us it was a gas chamber, we figure that what they turned the valve someplace and the gas came out of these shower heads.
That's what my-- my perception was that was not it at all.
They took-- there were chutes, uh, in the ceiling, in the roof of the building.

There were, say, like-- say like a post or a sieve-like sheet metal post that came from outside to inside with holes, perforations-- and the-- they would-- and there probably four or five, and I don't remember now.
There is a picture somewhere in there-- of even seeing these things.
But they-- but they were there.
And what they-- the way this guy had talked to some of the inmates, and-- and eventually I got to talk to this one guy.
And this was just before we left-- the day or so before we left.
The way they used the gas-- and I said-- I said, well, there's got to be a-- you know, where's the-- where's the faucet?
Where's the handle?
There has to be a handle they turn it on with.
I was thinking of, I don't know, carbon monoxide or something like that, some way to get gas inside that room, you know?
A pump or something.
He said hell no.
That's not what works.
He said, what they do-- those-- those shower heads don't go any place.
They just are fastened to the wall.
There's no pipes connected to them at all.
And-- and this man that explained it to him, who spoke English and showed it to me too, he said the same thing.
We went back in and-- and looked at it again, and he couldn't-- if you didn't really inspect it, you wouldn't even notice this.
But you can-- then he showed us the shaft, and he said they'd-- they take-- the-- the guy, the SS man has a mask on, has a gas mask on, and protective gloves and everything, and a screwdriver.
And the Zyklon B comes in a can-- a canister with a lid that you have to pry off like a paint can.
It's pressurized, you know what I mean?
It has-- you can't get it off accidentally.
You have to pry it all the way around like a can of paint.
And then he dumps the crystals in these slots, which drop down into the inside.
And as soon as they hit the air, they release the cyanide gas.
And that's what kills people.
And that's how this one worked.

And that's how all of them worked except for the ones that used decompression or, uh, carbon monoxide gas, which was the exhaust from airplane engines-- airplane motors, gas tanks, motors, truck motors, things of that nature.
Well, that's the story of the Dachau gas chamber.
Controversy-wise, some people say it was never used.
I argue on the internet with, uh, with deniers.
I have read books by scholars who say it was used, not like Auschwitz or not even-- not even like Mauthausen.
Mauthausen had a death gas chamber that they used to kill people with on purpose.
The story of Dachau gas chamber is they used it to experiment with on animals.
They used it to experiment to get rid of crazy people.
If you were-- if you went nuts, they put you in the gas chamber.
They could have just as well have shot you.

But there are letters published in-- in, uh, scholarly books, letters between Himmler and, uh, officers at Dachau, doctors-- about experimenting with gas at Dachau and taking-- using it on human beings.
Uh--
Yeah.

I didn't see that swing again until I got to-- now I never did see that swing again, but you-- you had-- you had the-- you had the-- the chai-- the cuffs with the real heavy, wide steel cuffs that they bolted them on, you know?
And about this much chain in between, so your hands were like this.
And they would put your hands behind you and do this.
They would do-- they had these poles at Dachau.
There-- there were more poles at Dachau where they used with the arms behind them.
There probably, I don't know, there was a whole row of them, and I had seen-- if I hadn't have known what they were from-- from Buchenwald, I would have had to ask somebody.
These were four by fours or bigger, and, uh, 10 or 12 feet tall, and they had a rope and a pulley just like a well-- the old-fashioned water well.
And they would put this-- these manacles, steel things on the guy's arms, and then hook it onto the chain and just pull him on up there.
And I've got some pictures of that, too.
And I just-- it's-- and let them hang there till they died.
Talk about crucifixion being bad.
It couldn't have been this bad.
Probably three days.
Now I'm going to say something, and I hope this-- I'm going to use the F word, and I didn't say this.
When we left, this-- there was a colonel that I had flown-- that had flown in with somebody else and was going to fly back with me, and I don't even remember why.
And, um, I had pre-flighted the airplane and everything was ready to go.
And we-- we say, he says let's have another-- one more smoke.
So we're standing there.
And we had to get away from the airplane because of the-- the fuel, the gasoline.
So we were just standing 5, 10 feet away.
And he was telling me some other stuff that he had found out that I didn't know about by-- by going around with these other guys.
And some of the stuff they were-- and had to write a report, uh, to, uh, General Eisenhower and so on.
And he was just furious.
He was-- he was-- uh, had a real white circle around his mouth, and his hands were shaking, and I-- the guy was just almost beside himself.
And I was-- by then, I was pretty calm.
I was very angry, I mean, and just on the edge of a rage, but this guy was in a rage.

And when he tried to smoke the cigarette, it was, you know, Jesus, I said, I hope he doesn't-- and some of these guys smoked in the airplane, and I hated-- you're not supposed to do that, you know, because it's a fabric airplane, and with wooden floors, and stuff.
It's not too cool to smoke in those damn airplanes.
So anyhow, we get-- we get in the airplane and I'm going to start the engine and there's, you know, there's a guy out there with a fire extinguisher.

And, um, before I said to the-- to the, uh, to guy with the fire extinguisher, who happened to be a tanker, this general, or this colonel said to me, you know-- and he was almost talking to himself-- he said, I wonder how many more of these fucking places we're going to find?
And I didn't say anything.
I just fired it up and we took off and went back to Frankfurt, and I never saw him again.
But I thought about that a lot, what, you know, what a shock it must've been to him to say something like that.
And-- because I felt like screaming, you know, and pounding on something or doing something physical, like I said.
The tendency is-- I don't know if women are like it or not.
Men tend to want to cause pain and do physical.
I think it's macho baloney, but I think that's what we are.
OK.
Question.
Oh well, we had-- that's a good question.
Shafe ?] had them.
Shafe ?] had them, and I don't-- they had aerial photographs, too, of a lot of these places, of Auschwitz and-- and other places in Poland.
And-- but we were given charts, just navigational charts, of-- so we can navigate from point A to point B.
That was all we needed.

We didn't need maps of-- you know-- we didn't need maps of-- all we needed was navigational charts that show the hills and the-- the altitude of the hills around if you're in the hills, and so you know if you're at 6,000 feet and the chart says that mountain is 7,000 feet, you better not go near that mountain, or else climb up a bit.
That's the way navigational charts are.
Went back to Frankfurt.
I went back to, uh, to Gelnhausen.
No, I didn't go back to Gelnhausen until later.
I went back to Frankfurt, and, yeah, we went-- I went to Gelnhausen because we weren't going to go anyplace for a while.
Or we were told, you know, a couple of days.
We were only, you know, 40 miles away.
And I took all this flight gear that I had, and I took-- I flew the L5, went back to the strip, and all these guys were asking me, where the hell -- ?
And I didn't want to talk to them about this.
You know, I said, really, it's the most horrible thing you've ever seen.
And then I started bad mouthing the Germans, you know.
And we got a-- you know, we still-- the war still going on, and goddamn, I want to get back into-- into combat and do some more shooting up and blah, blah, blah.
Just crazy talk.
And these guys picked up on it right away, too, because they knew, you know, coming from me and Snyder, the other guy that-- from our squadron, from our squadron that was with me.
And he was a drinker.
He was-- he was drunk.
When he wasn't flying, he was drunk.
I mean drunk.
Asleep drunk.
When he was flying, he was cool and good, but when he wasn't flying, when he was back at the-- at-- at Frankfurt, he was drunk.
You couldn't have-- you could have stuck him in a tub of hot water and he wouldn't have known it.
He wouldn't care.
But-- but he told some of the, you know, the guys, you guys wouldn't believe us if we told you.

There were a couple of days I took my flight suit that I had on at-- at Dachau, and I had a pair of $50 Wellington boots, handmade for me in London-- and these were-- these were ego trip fighter pilot boots-- and my flight-- my pinks and my flight suit and my jacket, that I have a picture over there-- but that's a different one.
Uh, the, you know, trousers.
You have-- OD-- it's just, uh, OD pants.
They're a little bit on the pink side.
They called them pinks.
They had grays and pinks and ODs.
And for some reason I had a pair of pinks on at some point in time in this thing, which were the ones that were-- that I thought smelled so bad.
So I took them out to my crew chief and I said, Louis, can we-- we used carbon tetrachloride a lot.
For-- you know what that is?
It's a real strong chemical that-- that, uh, evaporates almost instantly, but it's very deadly, which we didn't know at the time.
I mean, if you absorbed it in your skin, it gets in your liver and it stays there.
Whatever carbon tetrachloride I have ever handled in my whole life is still in my liver.
It stays there, and if you get too much it kills you.
But anyhow, I said, Louis, I want to-- well, we used to use it for cleaning our uniforms and cleaning tools and stuff, too.
It cut grease like nobody's business, and it had a medicinal type odor to it.
So I said, I want to clean my, uh, my flight suit, my pinks, and my, uh, underwear and my-- and my B-8 jacket with carbon tet.
We had it in these big cans.
So he said, OK.
We got a big can and-- and put all the stuff in there and we sloshed it around.
And carbon tet does not burn.
It is also is a flame retardant, and some extinguishers have carbon tetrachloride in them and it puts out-- it smothers a fire.
A small fire.
It's not any good anymore because they have better stuff.
So anyhow, we sloshed this stuff around and I hung it on the fence, and I told Louis about the smell.
I said, do you smell that, Louis?
That smells like shit?
Smells like dead dogs, dead--
He said, no, it smells kind of bad, but I don't smell that smell.
I said, well, it's kind of-- so I hung it out on the fence there by the-- by the strip, and the next day I went out there and was going to have my uniform pressed.
We had some German women that we paid them with coffee or something and they would press our uniforms for us.
And, um-- I still smell-- I said, Louis, do you smell this?
He said, no, I don't smell-- it doesn't even smell like carbon tet, because it evaporates.
And when you've been inside of a dry cleaners, you can-- there's a certain chemical odor.
Well, there was a faint odor of that.
But anyhow, I said, the hell with it.
Get me some-- get me some 100-octane gasoline.
What are you going to do?
I said, I'm going to burn this shit.
He says, your boots?
And I said, yeah.

So I took all this, the-- the flight suit, my pinks, my belt, my socks, my underwear, everything that had been in this carbon tet, and I put it all in a pile and poured gasoline on it and threw a match on it and burned it.
And, OK, that takes care of that.
I'm not going to smell that again.
But I still smelled it.
And Louis says, boy, I don't know about you.
His name was Louis Nardini.
He lives in Pittsburgh.
I talk to him on the phone all the time.
Little Italian guy.
He said, I don't know about you, Lieutenant.
And he said-- and he smoked cigars.
Smoke one of these stogies and whatever is wrong, that will fix you right up.
And I couldn't stand those damn cigars.
I went--
We were scheduled to go back out, so I-- but I went to the flight surgeon, who I knew, and he's the one  to give us our six-months physical.
And I told him, I said, you know, we've been through some of these Nazi camps.
And I said, you can't believe.
He said, well, I've talked to some of the other guys.
He said, you having trouble with the smell?
I said, oh yeah.
And he said, well, you're not the only one.
He said-- I said, I burnt my damn Wellington boots.
And he thought that was hilarious. $50, because they only made them custom made in the, you know, in the shoe shop.
He said, you burnt your Wellington boots.
Well shit, you're not going to get to go back to England to get another pair.
But he said, let me-- sit down.
And he said, that is in your head.
That smell will always be in your head.
As time goes on, it'll fade away.
But you can always, if you think about that, that smell, you can smell it.
You can bring it back just by thinking about it.
And he's right, I can.
And the first time I did was when I-- when Marian and I were on that panel.
When I started talking about that damn smell, and-- and it's on the tape that you saw.
When my-- I don't know whether you got to see it all or not.
But I told a story about that-- about that smell then, and-- and-- and OK, I'm digressing.
Yes.
Um, the next assignment was, uh, Bergen-Belsen, which was liberated by the British.
Had been liberated.
We had-- we had been to Bergen-- I had been to Bergen-Belsen once to deliver somebody there, and I don't-- some medical officer, American.
And I think there were two or three airplanes that went.
And these were-- they were really looking for doctors who had some kind of expertise in typhus and some of the diseases that were rampant through the camps.
So I didn't get to see much of Bergen-Belsen.
I dropped them off.
We went, you know, walked around the camp, and it blew me away completely, because all I saw was women.
What year was that?
What date?
Oh, God, it was, uh-- it was, uh-- between-- it was between the 15th of April and the 29th of April.
And like I say, it was-- I went up and back in the same day.
I didn't stay there overnight.
I just delivered somebody, and I think there were three of us that went in and we didn't even get a complete tour.
But they were-- what we did see were-- were SS people, where they're still in uniforms, and civilians from the town carrying bodies, and not allowed to drag them.
The British soldiers all over the place and a bunch of tough dudes, I'll tell you.
And they were making-- they had dug these huge-- now we're talking about pits now, that-- twice as deep as this room.
This house would fit, uh, two or three houses like this in there.
And these people had-- were going down in the pits and laying these corpses.
And these corpses were full of fecal matter, uh, blood, you know.
I mean, to touch one would almost be enough to make you vomit.
But that's what they made him do.
I hear-- yeah, there were mostly women that I saw the first time.
And I went back 10 days later with some other-- some other people, or a week later, something like that.
And by then, they-- the decision had been-- they were no longer carrying the corpses.
They were bulldozing them and throwing them in.
No.
These were-- these were civilians from the village-- villages around there, and the British to handle this.
They were in charge of Bergen-Belsen.
They insisted, the British non-coms, that these bodies be carried either two people or one person.
And some of them were little people, you know, 80 pounds, 60 pounds a dead person.
That's not very much.
But you couldn't-- they wouldn't let them drag them.
My point is they made you-- made you pick them up and physically carry them, as opposed to dragging them by the heel.
And so that was what was happening the first time.
And I was only there maybe three or four hours and I flew back to Frankfurt or flew on my way back in Frankfurt.
And I went back, uh, five, six days later with another officer, and I think he was a medical officer, too, because this thing was really desperate.
And when we got back the second time, he went in and did his business, and I talked to some of these British guys.
And that one guy said, 26-- we had 26,000 deaths since you were here before.
He was a sergeant-- a British sergeant, a big-- And he said-- he said I killed one of them.
And I can't remember what he called the guy.
He said, I killed one of them.
And I can remember-- I said, you did?
He says, yeah.
And you know how the British talk about the bloody this, the bloody that and their slang.
He had the bloody [INAUDIBLE] or whatever.
He's dragging a-- a prisoner by the foot.
A dead-- a dead-- a dead whatever they called them, a corpse, by the foot.
And he said, I yelled at him to pick him up and he didn't pay-- and he was in civilian clothes.
He said he had a hat on, like a-- like a dress hat, and a-- and a black suit.
And I yelled at him, he said, and he ignored me.
He just kept walking on with his-- dragging this.
And he said, I got so mad, I went over there and hit him with my rifle butt and brained him on the spot.
I said, you brained him?
He says, brains all over the place.
And he was just happy as hell.
I said, well who was he?
And he says, I don't know.
Some people took him away in a cart.
I was going to throw him in the hole, he says, but some people, probably his family, took him out of the camp in a cart and probably buried him.
But he said, the guy was like-- you know, I'm standing there aghast.
And hitting a man with a rifle butt is-- is you can easily brain him if you know how to do it.
Uh, and that was-- that-- Bergen-Belsen, from the standpoint of-- of total blowing you away, of seeing this is-- this is where what you see is unbelievable.
To see bulldozers pushing thousands of corpses into deep holes, pouring lime on top of them-- on them-- layers and layers.
I mean these are not just one layer.
This is maybe there might be, you know, 20 people stacked on top of one.
They've quit stacking them now.
They're just in the hole.
And then bulldozing dirt on top.
And then they put a marker on each pit.
There's 9 or 10 pits.
An estimate of how many people are in there, and there's over 100,000 that I know of, you know.
Now I've read some books about-- Bergen-Belsen is-- is-- there's not too much about that American-- in English for some reason.
There's a couple of books that tell you, but I've read, uh, books about-- what's the guy's name that-- that wrote 'The Holocaust'?
I've got several of his.
Mitchell-- uh, Gilbert-- Martin Gilbert.
I've corresponded with him, by the way.
He was Churchill's historian.
Wonderful man.
He's written probably 30 books about the Holocaust.
Well, they were set-- they had set up field hospitals, and they were-- they had brought-- there was no water.
There had been no water to the camp for two weeks is what I was told.
No food.
Here again there were warehouses with food.
The SS, they had all-- they had captured 40, 50 SS, women included, and the commandant of the camp.

And his biggest complaint was that he-- the water in the river was so-- the river was so full of dead bodies that it was contaminated, and he couldn't use the water to water-- to provide water for the inmates.
So they were without water for a couple of weeks, without food for a couple weeks.
Everybody-- all the SS were healthy looking, fat.
I've got pictures of them, you know?
All around this area were farms.
There were ware-- within six miles of this camp was a bakery that could bake 6,000 loaves of bread a day.
And they-- this, well, this is all second-hand stuff.
I didn't talk to anybody that was an official there, but I have tried to make a study of it, because one of the most frustrating things was that-- is that they just let them die.
They just didn't give a damn.
One of the subordinates to the commandant said-- and he was the doctor-- you know, there must be something we can do to get water, at least get some water.
And he said, I don't give a damn.
Let them die.
Now-- and of course this guy, to save his ass, told the British liberators that this is-- this is what he said.
Because the British had water to them that same day they liberated the camp.
Beg your pardon?
Didn't see any crematoria.
I didn't see-- no, no crematorias, no gas chambers.
Uh, lots of barracks, lots of people laying around on the ground, very sick.
Uh, they-- they were tending to people as fast as they could.
They had-- they had American medical personnel.
They had English personnel-- medical personnel.
They had French and Belgian medical personnel.
And they were just totally overwhelming.
What they did was they moved them all out of there into the cities, the towns around, and they burnt the whole thing to the ground.
That they-- and it was all burned.
Bergen-Belsen was burned, leveled to the ground.
